Facts.text: St. Paschal I begins his reign as Catholic Pope, succeeding Stephen IV
Giovanni Caetani, Chancellor to Pope Paschal II, is elected as his successor, takes the name of Pope Gelasius II
Matthias I Corvinus chosen King of Hungary
Mughal emperor Humayun trips and falls down the stairs of his library, dies three days later [or 20 Jan]
Abdij Church in Middelburg is destroyed by fire
Amsterdam merchant Hans Bontemantel baptized
Dutch mariners Jacob Le Maire and Willem Schouten are the first Europeans to discover Le Maire Strait, Tierra del Fuego - go on to round Cape Horn
Connecticut colony organizes under Fundamental Orders
Parliamentary army wins battle of Nantwich, Cheshire, English Civil War
Lord Baltimore's representative Margaret Brent ejected from the Maryland Council after requesting right to vote
Duke of Orleans joins Fronde rebels
First Jewish doctor in North American colonies, Jacob Lumbrozo, arrives in Maryland
Edward Wigglesworth appointed 1st north American divinity professor (Harvard)
German leaders elect Charles VII, Prince Elector of Bavaria, Holy Roman Emperor
A fire at Harvard University destroys Governor Winthrop's Telescope and its library of 5,000 books is mostly lost
French La Pérouse expedition arrives in Botany Bay, Australia, meeting the newly arrived "First Fleet" penal colony
1,500 New Mexican Indians & Mexicans defeated by US Colonel Price
University of Calcutta is founded as the first full-fledged university in South Asia
Political union of Moldavia and Wallachia; Alexandru Ioan Cuza is elected as ruler
Federal troops from Fort Monroe are sent to Fort Pikens on Santa Rosa Island near Pensacola, Florida
Romanian principality arises under King Alexander Cuza. Bucharest proclaimed its capital.
Gen J van Swieten conquers Kraton Atjeh, after thousands die
Revolutionary Vera Zasulich shoots at Fyodor Trepov, Governor of Saint Petersburg
World's oldest wooden sculpture the Shigir Idol - radiocarbon dated to 12,500 years old, is discovered in a peat bog near Kalata, Middle Urals
Battle of Mengo, Uganda: French missionaries attack British missionaries
Belgium government of Vandenpeereboom forms
Rubber heel for boots or shoes is patented by American Humphrey O'Sullivan
Battle of Spion Kop: South African Boers defeat the British army after it attempts to cross the Tugela River and relieve the besieged city Ladysmith
Newcastle Badminton Club, world's oldest, forms in England
Denmark and the US sign a treaty under which Denmark will sell the Danish West Indies to the USA for $5 million, but the sale will be postponed until 1917
Emily Hobhouse views the British administrated concentration camp at Bloemfontein for women and children
Victor Herbert's second opera "Madeleine" premieres at the Metropolitan Opera, NYC, with Frances Alda in the title role
German-British sea battle at Dogger Bank & Helgoland
The Military Service Bill, calling for conscription of men for war services, passes in the British House of Commons
Paris Conference on reparations is held
-54°F (-48°C), Danbury, Wisconsin (state record)
Eskimo Pie patented by Christian Kent Nelson of Iowa (not an Eskimo)
Lehman Caves National Monument established
Aztec Ruins National Monument in New Mexico established
Russian city of St Petersburg renamed Leningrad (changed back in 1991)
Moving picture of a solar eclipse taken from dirigible over Long Island
John Mills scores New Zealand's 1st Test century in cricket, sharing a record 276 run opening stand with Stewie Dempster
Albert Sarraut becomes Prime Minister of France
30,000 killed by earthquake in Concepcion Chile
Spanish government moves to Figueras
British troops march into Abyssinia
Jewish patients, nurses and doctors incinerated atAuschwitz-Birkenau
Allied troops occupy Nettuno Italy
Scottish 52nd Lowland division occupies Heinsberg
NFL adds 5th official, the back judge and allows sudden death in playoffs
Dutch Liberal Party forms - People's party for Freedom & Democracy (VVD)
Dutch government Drees-van Schaik resigns
Fire in main building of French Port Martin Antarctic base
First NFL team in Texas; Dallas Texans, formerly NY Yanks; loses 11 of 12 games
Photography exhibition "The Family of Man" curated by Edward Steichen opens at MOMA, New York, "greatest photographic enterprise ever undertaken"
96.5 cm precipitation at Kilauea Plantation, Hawaii (state record)
An inquiry considers building homes in a war devastated area in London
After warming to 100,000,000 degrees, 2 light atoms are bashed together to create a heavier atom, resulting in 1st man-made nuclear fusion
Dmitri Shostakovich's operetta "Moscow, Cheryomushki" premieres in Moscow
WHCT TV channel 18 in Hartford, CT (IND) begins broadcasting
Algeria uprises against French President de Gaulle
Lazard Brothers Ltd draw a check for $334,867,807.68
28 refugees escape from East to West Germany
Jackie Robinsonis 1st African American elected to Baseball's Hall of Fame; Bob Feller is also elected
24th Amendment to the US Constitution "The Elimination of Poll Taxes" goes into effect, removing states ability to deny voting rights due to failure to pay taxes
CBS purchases 1964 and 1965 NFL TV rights for $28.2 million
Dutch comic strip "Eric de Noorman" by Hans G. Kresse ends
117 passengers are killed after an Air India Boeing-707 plane crashes into Mont Blanc, France
WDIO TV channel 10 in Duluth, MN (ABC) begins broadcasting
Operation Coburg begins, an Australian and New Zealand military action during the Vietnam War
Students protest the erection of steel gates around the London School of Economics
Valeri Muratov skates world record 500m (38.99 sec)
Japanese Sergeant Shōichi Yokoi is found hiding in the jungles of Guam, where he had been since the end of World War II
WRIP (now WDSI) TV channel 61 in Chattanooga, TN (IND) 1st broadcast
10th British Commonwealth Games open in Christchurch, New Zealand
"Hot l Baltimore" situation comedy premieres on ABC TV
Fastest Earth-bound object, 7200 kph, in vacuum centrifuge, England
Cleveland Cavaliers score their biggest margin of victory to date (43 points), when they beat Milwaukee 132-89
Massacre of Atocha in Madrid: Five labor lawyers murdered by fascists in Madrid during the Spanish transition to democracy
Carter Executive Order on Intelligence (# 12036)
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
15th Space Shuttle (51-C) Mission-Discovery 3 is launched
Leon Brittan, Trade and Industry Secretary under Thatcher is 2nd cabinet minister to resign after 'Westland affair'
South Yemen Premier Haydar Bakr al-Attas becomes interim-president
Cerebral Palsy telethon raises $21 million
NBC premiere of fact based "The Murder of Mary Phagan"
First reported case of AIDS transmitted by heterosexual oral sex
14th Commonwealth Games open in Auckland, New Zealand
Japanese MUSES-A (Hiten) launched towards moon
LAs' Pat Riley becomes the 13th and fastest coach to reach the 500-victory plateau as the Lakers down the Indiana Pacers, 120-111; Riley (500-184) surpasses Don Nelson (500-317) to reach milestone
Chalino Sánchezis attacked and shot while performing at the Plaza Los Arcos restaurant in Coachella, California
14th annual star-athon $24,000,000
Polish ferry boat MS Jan Heweliusz sinks while crossing the Baltic Sea, killing 55 people
Soyuz TM-16 launches
Turkish journalist and writer Uğur Mumcu is assassinated by a car bomb in Ankara
Dow Jones Industrial Average closes above 3,900 for 1st time (3,914.48)
The Swiss government and several banks and businesses agree to create a memorial fund for victims of the Holocaust
Northern Ireland Secretary Peter Mandelson resigns
The United States Department of Homeland Security officially begins operation
Suicide bombing at Moscow's Domodedovo Airport kills 37 and injures 173
17 people are killed and 34 are injured in a bus crash in Taperas, Bolivia
A Japanese Coast guard ship engages a Taiwanese activist ship in the Senkaku Islands dispute
Russian police kill 13 rebels in Vedeno District, Chechnya
TV drama "The X-Files" returns after 13 years, reuniting lead actors David Duchovny and Gillian Anderson and produced again by Chris Carter
Former US Olympic team doctor Larry Nassar is found guilty of molesting over 150 girls and is sentenced to up to 175 years in prison
UK charity Presidents Club forced to close after revelations of sexual harassment at event revealed by the "Financial Times"
17 temperature records broken in South Australia including highest ever recorded for capital Adelaide at 46.6C

Search for missing Argentine footballer Emiliano Sala is called off after rescuers fail to find aircraft that disappeared from radar over English Channel 3 days earlier; search resumes funded by soccer community donations; wreckage discovered Feb 3
19 die in a fight and a fire at a nightclub in Sorong city, West Papua, Indonesia
At least 34 people killed and 65,000 left homeless after two different tropical storms batter Madagascar and Mozambique with Madagascar's capital Antananarivo particularly affected
Coup in Burkina Faso as military announce on TV they have overthrown President Roch Kaboré after his failure to stem an Islamic insurgency

Adani Enterprises owned by Gautam Adani, world's third richest man, loses $108billion in value after investment firm Hindenburg Research publishes report alleging stock manipulation and accounting fraud
Coldest winter in a decade in Afghanistan has killed at least 124 people and 70,000 livestock, leaving many areas cut off, according to state officials
Doomsday Clock reset 90 seconds until midnight, the closest it has ever been (set up 1947) by Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists, due partly to war in Ukraine
Peruvian Prime Minister Dina Boluarte call for a "national truce" after seven weeks of violent protests at Pedro Castillo's removal from office, which have left at least 50 people dead
Third baseman Scott Rolen (Philadelphia Philleis, St. Louis Cardinals) elected to Baseball Hall of Fame
Israeli forces expand their ground offensive into central Gaza's urban refugee camps saying it will last "many months"
Russian military plane shot down by Ukrainian forces in Russia kills all 74 on board including 65 Ukrainian Prisoners of War, prompts accusations on both sides
Sri Lanka passes controversial law to regulate online content, with critics claiming it will limit free speech
UK confirms 3-year loan of Ghana's "crown jewels" belonging to the Asante monarch to the Manhyia Palace Museum, Kumasi, 150 years after they were looted
US PresidentDonald Trumpfires over a dozen independent Inspectors General, without the required 30-day advance notice citing reasons for the firing to Congress
Claudiussucceeds his nephewCaligulaas Roman Emperor after the latter's assassination by officers of the Praetorian Guard
Synod of Worms: German KingHenry IVfires Pope Gregory VII
In Netherlands, Duke of Alva declaresWilliam I of Orangean outlaw
Holy Roman EmperorFerdinand IIdeclares his former military leaderAlbrecht von Wallensteina traitor - later has him assassinated
Pierre Corneille's play "Oedipe" premieres in Paris
KingCharles IIdisbands the English Cavalier Parliament after 18 years - the longest of any English or British parliament
TsarPeter the Greatbegins civil system
Louis XVIof France issues edict calling for the convocation of the Estates-General, a general assembly not called since 1614
Charles Darwinelected Fellow of the Royal Society
Modest Mussorgsky's opera "Boris Godunov" premieres in St Petersburg, Russia
Camille Saint-Saëns' orchestral tone poem "Danse Macabre" premieres
Lieutenant GeneralRobert Baden-Powellpublishes "Scouting for Boys" as a manual for self-instruction in outdoor skills and self-improvement. The book becomes the inspiration for the Scout Movement.
Franz Kafkastops working on "Amerika"; it will never be finished
Benito Mussolinidisallows non-fascist work union
Dmitri Shostakovich's opera "Lady Macbeth of the Mtsensk District" is performed in Moscow; roundly criticized by the Communist Party and banned in the Soviet Union until 1961
Benny Goodmanand His Orchestra record "Stompin' at the Savoy" for Victor Records in Chicago, Illinois
Pierre Laval's second French government falls
Adventure film "Gunga Din", based on the poem byRudyard Kiplingand starringCary Grant,Victor McLaglen, andDouglas Fairbanks Jr.is released
Eddie Collins, Willie Keeler & George Sisler elected to Baseball Hall of Fame
"The Grapes of Wrath", directed byJohn Fordand based onJohn Steinbeck's novel of the same name, starringHenry Fondaand Jane Darwell, is released
Adolf Hitlerorders German troops at Stalingrad to fight to the death
Jackie Robinsonsigns highest contract ($35,000) in Dodger history
Edward Albee's one-act play "American Dream" premieres in NYC
Retail entrepreneurBrian Epsteinsigns a 5-year management contract with the Beatles
Deputy Prime Minister Brian Faulkner resigns from the Northern Ireland cabinet in protest at the lack of 'strong government' on the part of PMTerence O'Neill
Queen Julianaof the Netherlands appointed honorary citizen of Addis Ababa
Spanish dictatorGeneral Francoannounces state of emergency
Columbia Records releases "Paul Simon", the second solo studio album by American singer-songwriter Paul Simon; it includes singles "Mother and Child Reunion" and "Me and Julio Down by the Schoolyard"
Former world heavyweight boxing championGeorge ForemanKOs Ron Lyle in 5th round at Caesar's Palace, Las Vegas; Foreman knocked down twice in 4th; 'The Ring' Fight of the Year
Super Bowl XVI, Pontiac Silverdome, Detroit, MI: San Francisco 49ers beat Cincinnati Bengals, 26-21; MVP:Joe Montana, SF, QB
NHL New York IslandersMike Bossyscores his 1,000th point
After losing the federal election toStephen Harper,Paul Martinresigns as leader of the Liberal party
Walt Disney International andBob Igerannounce it is buying computer animation film studio Pixar for $7.4 billion
PopeBenedict XVIrescinds the excommunications of four bishops consecrated without papal consent in 1988 by Archbishop Marcel Lefebvre
"21" second studio album byAdeleis released (Grammy Award for Album of the Year 2012, Brit Award for British Album of the Year, 2011 Billboard Album of the Year)
President Trumpwithdraws the US from the Trans-Pacific Partnership Agreement (TPPA)
Thierry Henryis dismissed as head coach of Monaco
US government recognises Venezuelan opposition leader Juan Guaido as the country's President
James Marshall finds gold in Sutter's Mill in Coloma, California
First canned beer, "Krueger's Cream Ale," is sold by American company Krueger Brewing Co.
Apple Computer Inc. unveils its revolutionary Macintosh personal computer in a Super Bowl TV ad directed byRidley Scott
Voyager 2 makes 1st fly-by of Uranus (81,593 km), finds new moons
Richard Aungerville [Richard de Bury], English bishop, writer (The Philobiblon) and one of the first English book collectors, born near Bury St Edmunds, England (d. 1345)
Galeazzo Maria Sforza, Duke of Milan (1466-76), born in Fermo (d. 1476)
Charles Sackville, 6th Earl of Dorset, English poet, courtier to Charles II and patron of the arts, born in Essex, England (d. 1706)
John Vanbrugh, Dutch-English playwright (The Provoked Wife), born in London (d. 1726)
William Congreve, English dramatist (Love for Love), born in Bardsey, Egland (d. 1729)
Thomas Tanner, English bishop and antiquarian (Bibliotheca Britannico-Hibernica), born in Wiltshire, England (d. 1735)
Christian von Wolff, German philosopher, born in Breslau, Silesia (d. 1754)
Farinelli [Carlo Broschi], Italian castrato (considered one of the great opera singers), born in Andria, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1782)
Georg Friedrich Schmidt, German engraver and etcher (court artist for King Frederick of Prussia and Empress Elizabeth of Russia), born in Wandlitz (d. 1775)
Frances Brooke, English writer, wrote the first novel written in Canada (The History of Emily Montague), born in Claypole, England (d. 1789)
Pierre de Beaumarchais, French polymath and dramatist (The Marriage of Figaro, Barber of Seville), born in Paris, France (d. 1799)
Anne Horton, scandalous English Duchess of Cumberland and Strathearn, wife of Prince Henry, brother of George III, born in London (d. 1808)
Wybo Fijnje, Dutch politician, and journalist, born in Zwolle, Netherlands (d. 1809)
Andrew Ellicott, American surveyor who surveyed the District of Columbia and site of Washington D.C., born in Buckingham Township, Pennsylvania (d. 1820)
Johann Chrysostomus Drexel, German composer, born in Epfenhausen, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1801)
Louis Alexandre Andrault de Langeron, French general who fought for Russia against the Ottomans and Napoleon, born in Paris (d. 1831)
Anthony Christiaan Winand Staring, Dutch poet (Ode to Simplicity), born in Gendringen, Netherlands (d. 1840)[1]
Karl Moser, German composer and violinist, born in Berlin, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1851)
Ernst Theodor Amadeus Hoffmann, German Romantic writer, judge and composer (Undine), born in Königsberg, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1822)
Charles Ferdinand, Duke of Berry, son of French King Charles X, born in Versailles, France (d. 1820)
Elizabeth Alexeievna (Louise of Baden), German-born Russian Tsarina as consort to Tsar Alexander I, born in Karlsruhe, Baden (d. 1826)
Nicholas Mori, Anglo-Italian violinist and conductor, born in London, England (d. 1839)
Karl von Holtei, Silesian actor, playwright and author (Die Vagabunden), born in Poland (d. 1880)
Karl von Staudt, German mathematician (projective geometry), born in the Free Imperial City of Rothenburg, (d. 1867)
Edwin Chadwick, British social reformer (Poor law reform, sanitation movement), born in Manchester, England (d. 1890)
Abraham van Stolk, Dutch art collector (Atlas Van Stolk in Rotterdam), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1896)
John Milton Thayer, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Bellingham, Massachusetts (d. 1906)
Adam Jacoby Slemmer, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Montgomery County, Pennsylvania (d. 1868)
Ferdinand Cohn, German biologist, born in Wrocław, Poland (d. 1898)
Karol Studzinski, Polish violinist and composer, born in The Free, Independent, and Strictly Neutral City of Cracow (d. 1883)
William Mason, American pianist, pedagogue, and composer, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1908)
John Pegram, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Petersburg, Virginia (d. 1865)
Vasily Surikov, Russian history painter (The Conquest of Siberia by Yermak Timofeyevich), born in Krasnoyarsk Russia (d. 1916)
Alexander Alexandrovich Ilyinsky, Russian composer (The Fountain of Bakhchisaray), born in Tsarskoye Selo, Russia (d. 1920)
Marguerite Durand, French journalist, feminist, and publisher (La Fronde), born in Paris, France (d. 1936)
Ethel Turner, Australian author (Seven Little Australians), born in Doncaster, England (d. 1958)
Konstantin Bogaevsky, Ukrainian-Russian Symbolist landscape painter, born in Feodosia, Russia (d. 1943)
Arthur Schomburg (Arturo Alfonso), Puerto Rican archivist of African American history and culture, born in Santurce, Puerto Rico (d. 1938)
Stanislaw Lyudkevich, Ukranian composer, born in Jaroslaw, Habsburg Empire (d. 1979)

Estelle Winwood [Goodwin], British-American stage and screen character actress (Quality Street; Darby O'Gill and the Little Peoples; The Producers), and oldest member of the Screen Actors Guild, born in Lee, Kent, England (d. 1984)
Henry King, American film director (Song of Bernadette, Gunfighter), born in Christiansburg, Virginia (d. 1982)
Ernst Heinrich Heinkel, German aeronautical engineer (1st rocket-powered aircraft), born in Grunbach, Germany (d. 1958)
Hedwig "Vicki" Baum, Austrian American author (Men Never Know, Grand Hotel), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1960)
Walter Model, German WWII field marshal and panzer commander (Battle of the Bulge), and WW1 junior officer (Second Battle of the Marne), born in Genthin, Germany (d. 1945)
Albin Zollinger, Swiss poet and writer (Der Halbe Mensch), born in Zürich, Switzerland (d. 1941)
Eugen Roth, German poet, born in Munich (d. 1976)
E. A. Speiser, American Assyriologist and Bible scholar (Anchor Bible Genesis), born in Skalat, Galicia (then in Austrian Poland (d. 1965)
Oskar Morgenstern, German-American economist, born in Görlitz, Germany (d. 1977)
Walter Keirnan, American TV panelist (I've Got a Secret), born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 1978)
Robert Gwathmey, American social realist artist (Arts & Letters), born in Manchester, Virginia (d. 1988)
Dorothy Patten, American actress, born in Chattanooga, Tennessee (d. 1975)
J. Howard Marshall [James Howard Marshall II), American businessman and husband of Anna Nicole Smith, born in Germantown, Pennsylvania (d. 1995)
Norman Cook, British museum curator and co-founder of the Museum of London, born in Maidstone, England (d. 1994)[1]
Jean Daetwyler, Swiss composer, chiefly of music for the alpenhorn, and music educator, born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1994)
Maurice Couve de Murville, French politician (Foreign Minister and Prime Minister of France 1968-69), born in Reims, France (d. 1999)
Ann Todd, English stage and screen actress (The Seventh Veil; The Paradine Case; The Passionate Friends), born in Hartford, England (d. 1993)
Jean R. Yawkey, American baseball executive (owner Boston Red Sox 1976-92), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1992)
Tiny Winters [Frederick Gittens], English jazz bassist and singer, born in London (d. 1996)
C. L. Moore, American science fiction & fantasy writer (Judgment Night), born in Indianapolis, Indiana (d. 1987)
Ken Weekes, West Indian cricket batsman (2 Tests, top score 137; first Test cricketer born in US), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1998)
Norman Dello Joio [Nicodemo DeGioio], American classical composer (Choreography: Three Dances for Strings), born in New York City (d. 2008)
Robert Motherwell, American painter (Elegies to the Spanish Republic), born in Aberdeen, Washington (d. 1991)
Vitezslava Kapralova, Czech composer, born in Brno, Moravia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1940)
Jack Brickhouse, American sports broadcaster, born in Peoria, Illinois (d. 1998)
John Corner, British mathematician, born in Newcastle upon Tyne, United Kingdom (d. 1996)
Marvin Creamer, American mariner, 1st recorded person to sail around the world without navigational instruments, born near Vineland, New Jersey (d. 2020)
Walter Haas Jr., American businessman and sports team owner (Levi Strauss & Co, Oakland A's), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1995)
Gottfried von Einem, Austrian composer (Dantons Tod), born in Bern, Switzerland (d. 1996)
John McLiam [Williams], Canadian actor (First Blood, Sleeper, Men From Shiloh), born in Alberta, Canada (d. 1994)
Oral Roberts, American Televangelist, founder Oral Roberts College, born in Pontotoc County Oklahoma (d. 2009)
Leon Kirchner, American contemporary classical composer (Pulitzer Prize for Music 1967), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 2009)
Nora Beloff, English journalist and author, born in London (d. 1997)
Don Saddler, American choreographer & dancer (Holiday Hotel), born in Van Nuys, California (d. 2014)
Jerry Maren [Gerard Marenghi], American actor (The Wizard of Oz; Mayor McCheese), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2018)
Jimmy Forrest, American jazz saxophonist (Night Train), born In St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1980)
Bernard J. Dwyer, American politician (Rep-D-NJ, 1981- 93), born in Perth Amboy, New Jersey (d. 1998)
Charles Socarides, American psychiatrist, born in Brockton, Massachusetts (d. 2005)
Neil Franklin, English soccer defender (26 caps; Stoke City, Hull City, Crewe Alexandra) and manager (Colchester United), born in Shelton, England (d. 1996)
Simeon ten Holt, Dutch minimalist composer (Canto Ostinato), born in Bergen, Netherlands (d. 2012)
David Craighead, American organist and educator, born in Strasburg, Pennsylvania (d. 2012)
John Spencer, Earl of Spencer, English peer and father of Diana, Princess of Wales, born in London, England (d. 1992)
Marvin Kaplan, American character and voice actor (Top Cat - "Choo Choo"; Alice - "Henry"), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 2016)
Robert Kastenmeier, American politician (Rep-D-WI, 1959-91), born in Beaver Dam, Wisconsin (d. 2015)
William Hudson, American actor (I Led 3 Lives), born in Gilroy, California (d. 1974)
Clyde Adler, American writer and voice actor (Soupy Sales Show - "White Fang"; "Black Tooth"), born in Oak Park, Illinois (d. 1993)
Georges Lautner, French director and screenwriter, born in Nice, France (d. 2013)
Raymond Jacobs, American Marine Corp sergeant (one of the WWII Iwo Jima flag planters), and news journalist, born in Bridgeport, Connecticut (d. 2008)
Robert Delpire, French publisher, editor and photographer, born in Paris, France (d. 2017)
Alexander Polikoff, American author and lawyer known for work on the Gautreaux v. Chicago Housing Authority court case, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2025)[1]
Jerry Burns, American football coach (University of Iowa 1961-65; Minnesota Vikings 1986-91), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2021)
Paula Hawkins, American politician (Sen-R-FL, 1981-86), born in Salt Lake City, Utah (d. 2009)
Desmond Morris, English zoologist (Human Ape; Body Language), born in Purton, England
Michel Serrault, French actor (Nelly & Monsieur Arnaud, La Cage aux Folles), born in Brunoy, France (d. 2007)
Sid Green, British screenwriter, with Dick Hills (Two Of A Kind; The Strange World of Gurney Slade), and comedian (Those Two Fellers), born in London, England (d. 1999)
Bernard Matthews, English turkey farmer (Bernard Matthews Farms), born in Brooke, England (d. 2010)
Ib Nørholm, Danish organist, composer (Aspects of Sand and Simplicity), choral conductor, and educator, born in Søborg, Gladsaxe (d. 2019)[1]
Maria Piątkowska, Polish athlete (WR 4 x 100m relay 1964), born in Goleni, Moldova (d. 2020)
Éliane Radigue, French composer of electronic music (Trilogie de la Mort), and acoustic works (Occam series), born in Paris
Taylor Reed, American stage and screen actor (Easy Money), born in Arizona (d. 1993)
Zeke Carey, American doo-wop and R&B singer (The Flamingos - "I Only Have Eyes For You"), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1999)
Burdette Haldorson, American basketball forward (Olympic gold 1956, 1960; University of Colorado Boulder), born in Austin, Minnesota (d. 2023)
Stanisław Grochowiak, Polish poet, born in Leszno, Poland (d. 1976)
(Arthur) Bamber Gascoigne, English television presenter (University Challenge, 1962-87), and author, born in London, England (d. 2022)
Eric Ashton, English rugby league footballer, born in St Helens, United Kingdom (d. 2008)
Daniel Goode, American composer, and clarinetist (Gamelan Son of Lion), born in New York City
Don Bosseler, American College Football HOF fullback (University of Miami; Pro Bowl 1959 Washington Redskins), born in Wethersfield, New York (d. 2024)
Doug Kershaw, American Cajun, folk and bluegrass fiddle player, born in Cameron Parrish, Louisiana
Jack Scott, Canadian rockabilly singer-songwriter ("My True Love", "No One Will Ever Know"), born in Windsor, Ontario (d. 2019)
Robert Downey, Sr [Elias Jr], American filmmaker (Putney Swope; Rittenhouse Square), and actor (You Gotta Walk It Like You Talk It or You'll Lose That Beat), born in New York City (d. 2021)
Bobby Beathard, American Pro Football Hall of Fame executive (GM Washington Redskins Super Bowl 1982, 87; San Diego Chargers), born in Zanesville, Ohio (d. 2023)
Gyula Torok, Hungarian boxer (Oly Gold 1960), born in Kispest, Hungary (d. 2014)
Julius Hemphill, American jazz saxophonist, and composer, born in Fort Worth, Texas (d. 1995)
Dave Getz, American drummer (Big Brother & The Holding Company), born in Brooklyn, New York City
Aaron Neville, American Grammy Award-winning R&B, soul, and pop singer, and keyboardist (Neville Brothers; "Don't Know Much"), born in New Orleans, Louisiana
Michael Chapman, British progressive folk-rock guitarist and singer-songwriter (Looking for Eleven; Fully Qualified Survivor), born in Leeds, England (d. 2021)
Gary Hart, American pro wrestler & manager (WWE HOF 2020), born in Ottawa, Kansas (d. 2008)
Olav Nilsen, Norwegian soccer striker/midfielder (62 caps; Viking FK), born in Stavanger, Norway (d. 2021)
Julie Bennett, American actress (Hey There It's Yogi Bear), born in Beverly Hills, California
Subhash Ghai, Indian film director, born in Nagpur, India
David Gerrold [Jerrold David Friedman], American science fiction screenwriter (Star Trek) and author (The Martian Child), born in Chicago, Illinois
Klaus Nomi, German singer, born in Immenstadt, Germany (d. 1983)
D. Todd Christofferson, American LDS apostle, born in American Fork, Utah
John Garamendi, American politician, born in Camp Blanding Joint Training Center, Florida
John Harrison, South Africa correspondent (BBC), born in Aldershot, England (d. 1994)
Michael Ontkean, Canadian actor (The Rookies, Slap Shot, Twin Peaks), born in Vancouver, British Columbia
David Randall, British priest and founder of AIDS/HIV center Cara, birthplace unknown (d. 1996)
Giorgio Chinaglia, Italian soccer striker (14 caps; Lazio, NY Cosmos), born in Carrara, Italy (d. 2012)
Masashi 'Jumbo' Ozaki, Japanese golfer (record 94 Japan Tour titles), born in Kaifu District, Tokushima
Stephen Barncard, American sound engineer (Wally Heider Studio, San Francisco), born in Kansas City, Kansas
Warren Zevon, American rock singer-songwriter, and piano player ("Werewolves of London"; "Lawyers, Guns and Money"; "Excitable Boy"), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2003)
Charlie Souza, American session and touring bass player and vocalist (Mudcrutch), born in Tampa, Florida
Elliott Abrams, American ass. secretary of state/supplied arms to the Contras, born in New York
Michael Des Barres, British rock singer (Detective; Power Station), actor (MacGyver; The New WKRP in Cincinnati), and radio DJ, born in Hove, England
Michio Kaku, American physicist, futurist, author (Physics of the Impossible; The God Equation: The Quest for a Theory of Everything), broadcaster, and peace activist, born in San Jose, California
Bart Gordon, American politician (Rep-D-TN, 1985-2011), born in Murfreesboro, Tennessee,
Benjamin Urrutia, Ecuadorian-born American writer, born in Guayaquil, Ecuador
Daniel Auteuil, French actor (Elegant Criminal, Manon of Spring), born in Algiers, Algeria
